hook: Cinema history hoarded inside the Lambeth workhouse where Chaplin was admitted as a boy
facts: Housed in the former master's house of the 1871-73 Lambeth Workhouse, now a Grade II listed building
Eight-year-old Charlie Chaplin was admitted to the workhouse in the 1890s when his mother faced destitution
Collection founded 1986 by Ronald Grant and Martin Humphries from their own hoard
Holds an estimated million-plus photos and roughly seventeen million feet of film
w: Eight-year-old Charlie Chaplin was admitted to this Lambeth workhouse when his mother went under. Decades on, two collectors filled the old master's house with ushers' uniforms, art deco seats, popcorn cartons and seventeen million feet of film. A shrine to the picture palace, in the building that nearly swallowed its most famous child.

hook: International-style flats designed by a 23-year-old, Bauhaus by way of Streatham
facts: Designed by Frederick Gibberd, built 1933-36 when he was only 23
218 flats in three blocks, Grade II* listed
Drew on German constructivism and the Bauhaus; reinforced concrete frame with cork insulation
Original communal facilities included roof terraces, a pool and a restaurant
w: A 23-year-old Frederick Gibberd dropped a slice of the Bauhaus onto Streatham Hill in 1936: 218 flats in white concrete, continuous balconies, roof terraces for sunbathing and a pool, all aimed at young moderns sick of digs. Constructivism for the commuter belt. The pool's covered over now, but the rhythm holds.

hook: A skeletal reaper strides over London hurling bombs, painted in 1981 against the Bomb
facts: Painted 1981 by Brian Barnes and Dale McCrea with residents of Carlton Mansions
Stands on Coldharbour Lane opposite Brixton Village
Depicts a giant skeleton draped in the flags of nuclear powers as a mushroom cloud erupts
Restored and re-revealed in June 2021
w: A colossal skeleton in the flags of the nuclear powers strides over the Thames, scattering bombs as a mushroom cloud blooms. Brian Barnes and the Carlton Mansions co-op painted it in 1981, deep in the second Cold War, when the four-minute warning felt real. A rare survivor of Brixton's mural decade. Death, draped in superpower colours.

hook: A green-domed clock tower on a traffic island, Stockwell's own cenotaph
facts: Unveiled 1922 on the triangular island where Clapham Road meets South Lambeth Road
Commemorates local men who fell in the First World War, later the Second added
Restored as a memorial garden; nearby a deep-shelter shaft carries a community mural
The mural shaft commemorates locals including Violette Szabo and Roger Moore
w: Marooned on a traffic island where Clapham Road forks, this green-domed clock tower of 1922 carries the names of Stockwell's war dead, the locals' own cenotaph. Round the corner a deep-shelter vent shaft wears a painted roll of honour to the neighbourhood, Violette Szabo and Roger Moore among them. Grief, planted at a junction.

hook: A concrete cathedral for buses, once Europe's largest unsupported roof span
facts: Opened April 1952, designed by Adie, Button and Partners with Thomas Bilbow
Roof of ten shallow arched concrete ribs with cantilevered barrel vaults and skylights
Built in concrete because of a postwar steel shortage
Grade II* listed in 1988; parks around 200 buses unobstructed
w: Postwar steel ran short, so the engineers cast the roof in concrete instead: ten shallow arched ribs sweeping over an unbroken floor that swallows 200 buses. When it opened in 1952 the span beat anything in Europe. Grade II* now, a cathedral nave with route numbers. Light pours through the skylight ridges onto the diesel.

hook: A bronze Kalachakra mandala laid out behind the Imperial War Museum's guns
facts: Opened and consecrated by the Dalai Lama in May 1999, over 3,000 attending
Sits in Geraldine Mary Harmsworth Park beside the Imperial War Museum
Centre holds the first bronze cast of the Kalachakra Mandala, set in black limestone
Tibetan title Samten Kyil, 'Place of Contemplation'
w: Behind the Imperial War Museum's naval guns, the Dalai Lama consecrated this circle in 1999. At its heart, the first bronze cast of the Kalachakra mandala, ringed by eight meditation seats for the eightfold path, with a pillar echoing a 9th-century Lhasa treaty stone. Contemplation, planted in the shadow of artillery. Quiet defiance.

hook: A salvaged portico and a Mendelssohn sundial, all that survives of Denmark Hill's lost mansions
facts: Opened 1907, named for John Ruskin, who grew up on Denmark Hill
The Portico shelter is the only fragment left of the demolished 170 Denmark Hill
A terracotta sundial marks Mendelssohn's 1842 stay at the vanished Dane House
Laid out by J.J. Sexby with pond, terraces, bandstand and Old English Garden
w: Grand villas once lined Denmark Hill; when they fell, the park kept their bones. A lone stone portico, swung around to face the lawns, marks where number 170 stood, and a terracotta sundial recalls Mendelssohn lodging at long-gone Dane House in 1842. Ruskin grew up here and wrote of its wildness. Faded gentility, mown into a public green.

n: The Old English Garden, Battersea Park
hook: A walled secret garden inside the park, replanted by a Chelsea gold medallist
facts: Built in the early 1900s, decades after Battersea Park itself opened
Walled enclosure with a lily pond, central fountain urn, arches and sundial
Redesigned by garden designer Sarah Price in 2012
Tended by the charity Thrive, working with disabled gardeners
w: Slip through the high brick walls and the city drops away: a lily pond, a fountain urn, climbing roses on iron arches, a sundial. Laid out around 1900, it fell to neglect until the charity Thrive and designer Sarah Price coaxed it back, gardened largely by disabled hands. A room of green walled off from the park's noise.

hook: A neo-Byzantine water palace dressed up to placate the man who sold the land
facts: Built 1894 on Conyers Road for the Southwark and Vauxhall Water Company
Designed by company engineer James William Restler in neo-Byzantine style
Ornamental at the freeholder's insistence as a condition of sale
Compared to Abbey Mills, the 'cathedral of sewage' in Stratford
w: The landowner would only sell on one condition: whatever they built must look handsome. So the water company gave Streatham a striped neo-Byzantine palace in 1894, banded brick and arched windows, to hide the pumps inside. Pure municipal vanity, like Abbey Mills downriver. A waterworks pretending to be a basilica.

hook: Terraced spa gardens on the site of the mineral wells that made Streatham fashionable
facts: Mineral springs found here in 1659 turned Streatham into a spa village
An original well head and a cascade of ponds survive in the gardens
Bought by public subscription and opened to the public in 1913
Grade II listed garden with an Old English Garden and a White Garden
w: In 1659 someone found medicinal springs up here and Streatham briefly became a spa, gentry trooping out to take the waters. The wells faded, the big house went, but the terraced gardens stayed, an original well head still set among the cascading ponds. Saved by subscription in 1913. A vanished resort, gone to seed and azaleas.

hook: London's last working toll gate, still charging cars to cross a private road
facts: On College Road, the last surviving toll gate in London
Dates to 1789; most toll gates were abolished by Act of Parliament in 1864
Drivers are still charged to pass along the private road
Old toll boards survive listing rates for horses, donkeys, sheep and hogs
w: Parliament abolished London's toll gates in 1864, but this one on College Road never got the memo. Standing since 1789, it still levies a charge to drive the private road to Sydenham Hill, the last working tollgate in the capital. The old board survives, pricing oxen, donkeys and hogs by the score. A turnpike that simply refused to die.

hook: A Victorian girls' industrial home built to train the poor in domestic service
facts: Foundation stone laid 1890 by Princess Louise, Queen Victoria's daughter; opened 1891
Grew from the Ragged School movement to house and train destitute girls
Stands on Dartmouth Road beside Forest Hill Pools, Grade II listed
Later used as artists' studios and an early years centre
w: Queen Victoria's daughter laid the stone in 1890 for a home that took poor girls off the streets and trained them, scrubbing and sewing, for service downstairs. An offshoot of the Ragged Schools, dressed in red-brick respectability beside the baths. Charity with a stiff collar. Later it sheltered artists, not orphans.

hook: The church of the abolitionists, where Wilberforce's Clapham Sect plotted the end of slavery
facts: Built 1776 on the north side of Clapham Common
Spiritual home of the Clapham Sect, evangelical reformers and abolitionists
Members included William Wilberforce, Henry Thornton and Lord Teignmouth
The group coalesced under rector John Venn from 1792
w: Round this plain Georgian church in the 1790s gathered the Clapham Sect, rich evangelicals who lived nearby and decided slavery had to end. Wilberforce and Thornton worshipped here, drafting campaigns between sermons. Decades of pamphlets and parliamentary grind launched from a common-side pew. Quiet faith with a very long fuse.

hook: A refuge for strays founded by a woman the Times mocked, defended by Dickens
facts: Founded 1860 by Mary Tealby as the Temporary Home for Lost and Starving Dogs
Began in a scullery in Holloway before moving to Battersea in 1871
The Times ran a scathing attack on the idea in October 1860
Charles Dickens publicly took Tealby's side
w: Mary Tealby started it in her Holloway scullery in 1860, taking in the strays Victorian London left to starve. The Times sneered at the waste of fussing over dogs while the poor went hungry; Dickens leapt to her defence. The shelter outlived the mockery, crossing to Battersea in 1871. She died too soon to see it. Compassion, ridiculed into respectability.
